coredata.dc:title: On-site monitoring of L-adrenaline neurotransmitter in biological samples by chitosan oligosaccharide derived carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Sartho Immaculate Mercy, Joseph
Varatharajan, Pandiaraja
Hafiz Mamat, Mohamad
Kutti Rani, S.
Vasimalai, Nagamalai
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CDs) are fascinating fluorescent nanomaterial for spectrofluorimetry sensor applications, owing to the fact of its strong chemical stability, high quantum yield and high photostability. At this instance, we have developed the fluorescent sensing technique to detect the L-adrenaline (L-Adr) by nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as a sensing probe. The nitrogen-doped carbon dots was synthesized using chitosan oligosaccharide by hydrothermal method. The synthesized chitosan oligosaccharide derived CDs (COL-CDs) exhibits blue fluorescence under UV light and the size of the carbon quantum dots is 3.39 ± 0.08 nm calculated from HR-TEM image. The hormone called L-Adr found in the human body is crucial for controlling visceral processes. Utilizing the process of photo-induced electron transfer (PET), the previously mentioned COL-CDs have applied to sense the L-Adr. The sensing mechanism have explained clearly using Rehm-Weller equation by calculating ΔG. Tap water, blood serum, urine and adrenaline drug samples employed as real sample for the sensing L-Adr. A reagent free paper-based kit established for the purpose of on-site monitoring L-Adr. The smartphone’s colour picker software used to measure the RGB intensity from the paper-based kit.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots
Chitosan oligosaccharide
L-adrenaline
Fluorescent sensor
Paper based kit

Fabrication of reagent-less paper based sensor
Absorbance and emission spectral studies of COL-CDs
Sensing mechanism of COL-CDs in the presence of L-Adr
Reagent-less paper based-kit for the sensing of L-Adr

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) (a) Absorbance spectrum and (b) Emission spectrum of COL-CDs (B) 3D plot for the fluorescence spectra of COL-CDs obtained with various excitation wavelength ranging from 310-420 nm, Inset (A): Photographs of COL-CDs under (i) Day light and (ii) UV light.
(A) FT-IR spectra of (a) COL and (b) COL-CDs. (B) XRD pattern of COL-CDs.
HR-TEM image of COL-CDs, 20 nm magnification, Inset: (A) Crystal Lattice image of COL-CDs.

Emission spectra of COL-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of (a) 0, (b) 0.24, (c) 0.59, (d) 3.97, (e) 5.93, (f) 7.87, (g) 9.8, (h) 11.7, (i) 13.6, (j) 15.5, (k) 17.4, (l) 19.2, (m) 21.1, (n) 22.9, (o) 24.7 (p) 26.5 (q) 28.3 (r) 30.1 (s) 31.8 (t) 33.6 µM L-Adr. Inset: photographs (i) before and (ii) after the addition 33.6 µM L-Adr (iii) Linear range calibration curve.

(A) Normalized absorption spectrum and emission spectrum of COL-CDs, (B) cyclic voltammetry curve of COL-CDs, (C) cyclic voltammetry of COL-CDs in the presence of L-Adr and (D) Fluorescence lifetime results of COL-CDs and after the reaction with L-Adr.

Effect of interferences: (A) The relative fluorescence intensity of COL-CDs in the presence of 2 mM common interferences like L-dopa, dopamine, Nor-adrenaline, histamine, nicotinamide, creatinine, caffeine and 4 mM of biomolecules such as L-cystine, ascorbic acid, L-glutamine, uric acid, glucose, lysine, citric acid Vs 5.93 µM the concentration of L-Adr; (B) Corresponding photographs of COL-CDs in the presence of common interferences Vs 5.93 µM the concentration of L-Adr.
(A) Reagent less paper based kit for the detection L-Adr and (B) Corresponding linear plot of RGB intensity measured using colour picker app Vs different concentration of L-Adr.
Schematic representation for the synthesis of Chitosan Oligosaccharide Lactate derived carbon dots by hydrothermal method.
Schematic representation for the possible mechanism of L-Adr sensor using COL-CDs.
Calculated life time values for COL-CDs and COL-CDs in the presence of L-Adr.

row.entry: COL-CDs in the presence of L-Adr

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Real sample analysis for the sensing of L-Adr.

head.ce:title: On-site monitoring of L-adrenaline neurotransmitter in biological samples by chitosan oligosaccharide derived carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CDs) are fascinating fluorescent nanomaterial for spectrofluorimetry sensor applications, owing to the fact of its strong chemical stability, high quantum yield and high photostability. At this instance, we have developed the fluorescent sensing technique to detect the L-adrenaline (L-Adr) by nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as a sensing probe. The nitrogen-doped carbon dots was synthesized using chitosan oligosaccharide by hydrothermal method. The synthesized chitosan oligosaccharide derived CDs (COL-CDs) exhibits blue fluorescence under UV light and the size of the carbon quantum dots is 3.39 ± 0.08 nm calculated from HR-TEM image. The hormone called L-Adr found in the human body is crucial for controlling visceral processes. Utilizing the process of photo-induced electron transfer (PET), the previously mentioned COL-CDs have applied to sense the L-Adr. The sensing mechanism have explained clearly using Rehm-Weller equation by calculating ΔG. Tap water, blood serum, urine and adrenaline drug samples employed as real sample for the sensing L-Adr. A reagent free paper-based kit established for the purpose of on-site monitoring L-Adr. The smartphone’s colour picker software used to measure the RGB intensity from the paper-based kit.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, fluorescence nanomaterials have witnessed an exponential growth in various research field focused on their synthesis and application in various scientific and technological domains [1]. Among those fluorescent materials, carbon dots (CDs) often referred to “carbon quantum dots,” which are nanometer-sized particles showing fascinating physical and chemical properties [2]. They exhibit good optical properties [3], low toxicity [4], excellent biocompatible [5], facile functionalization [6] strong and tunable fluorescence emission, making them appealing for various fields such as photodynamic therapy [7], drug carriers [8], disease treatment [9], bio-imaging [10] and bio-sensing [10]. One-pot synthesis approach for CDs preparation have emerged as a promising method, which offered a tunable fluorescence and surface functional groups [11,12].

There are many methods available in the literature for the synthesis of carbon dots. Among them, hydrothermal method has gained prominence greatly valued over other bottom-up approaches, due to its simple operating system, low-cost apparatus and low temperature synthesis [13]. The method involves a controlled reaction of the precursor materials in the aqueous medium under elevated temperature and pressure [14]. The hydrothermally synthesized CDs materials have significant electronic and photonic properties and size dependent fluorescence properties owing to the quantum confinement [15].

Biosensors meets the quest for producing the portable sensing technologies for the on-site, rapid, selective and sensitive detection and monitoring of the biomolecules. L-Adrenaline or Epinephrine (L-Adr) belongs to the category of catecholamine, plays a pivotal role as an hormone, as well as neurotransmitters in human body [16]. When faced with a stressful situation or danger, the adrenal glands release epinephrine into the bloodstream, leading to a temporary increase in its concentration. Moreover, L-Adr is responsible for the “fight or flight” response and prepares the body for quick and intense physical action [17]. The specific levels of L-Adr in the bloodstream can vary depending on the individual, the situation, and any underlying medical conditions. Normal levels of L-Adr in the blood range from about 0.01 to 0.80 nmol/litre [18]. The abnormal level of L-Adr in the blood plasma or urine often indicates the pathological condition like tumors or serious neurological disorders [19]. The importance in detecting L-Adr for clinical diagnosis, the existing methods still requires improvements in terms of simplifications, flexibility and more over in the selectivity and sensitivity [20].

There are many routinely developed diagnostic method for the detection of L-Adr are available such as electrophoresis [21], liquid chromatography [21], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and circular dichorism [22]. These techniques have a limitation like, high-cost, time consuming, low selectivity and sensitivity. With these considerations, the fluorescent-based biosensors are an excellent alteration out of these methods. The spectrofluorimetry technique have worked based on the interaction between L-Adr and N-CDs, enables sensitive and selective detection through changes in fluorescence intensity of N-CDs. The binding interaction between L-Adr and N-CDs leads to alterations in fluorescence intensity, emission wavelength, or quenching behavior, allowing for precise quantification of L-adrenaline concentrations.

Chitosan Oligosaccharide Lactate (COL) is basically a chitin derivatives and possess good chemical stability and high positive charge density [23]. COL is utilized in this study as a precursor material to ensure effective formation COL-CDs. It can used as a probe for the detection of L-Adr with high selectivity and sensitivity.

Chitosan Oligosaccharide Lactate (COL), L-adrenaline (L-Adr), L-dopa, creatinine, dopamine, nor-adrenaline, histamine, caffeine, nicotinamide, L-cysteine, ascorbic acid, L-glutamine, uric acid, L-glucose, L-lysine were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. A phosphate buffer solution (pH 1.0 to pH 12) was prepared with monosodium and disodium phosphate salts (0.2 M). Further, pH adjusted with sodium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid. All glassware was washed thoroughly with (3:1 ratio of HCl/HNO3) aqua regia and was then rinsed with Milli-Q water prior to use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Chitosan Oligosaccharide Lactate derived COL-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent COL-CDs was synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method. Chitosan Oligosaccharide Lactate (COL) can be used as a carbon and nitrogen source for the preparation of CDs. The COL-CDs synthesis can be achieved through bottom up approaches by application of external energy. One such technique for synthesis is the hydrothermal method, which crystallizes substance in the sealed container at high temperature and pressure. In brief 0.05 g of COL mixed with 15 mL water and transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 10 hrs. After 10 hrs, the autoclave was allowed to cool for the room temperature. The solution which was colourless initially turned to light yellow transparent liquid. This indicates the successful formation of COL-CDs. The carbon dots were formed undergoing four consecutive stages like dehydration, polymerization, carbonization and passivation. Thus, COL undergoes these four stages, which it is transformed into COL-CDs (Scheme 1 ). Finally, the obtained solution was centrifuged at 6500 rpm for 15 min to eliminate the unreacted materials. The supernatant solution was collected and stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C for further analysis.

Fourier transform Infrared spectrometer (JASCO Rapid Scan FT/IR-6600) instrument was used to study the nature of the chemical bond and the presence of the various functional groups. High resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, JEM-2100) were used to obtain the size and morphological images of COL-CDs. Horiba Fluoromax spectrofluorometer was used to record the emission and excitation spectra. UV–Vis spectral data were monitored with the help of Lambda 35 PerkinElmer UV–Vis spectrophotometer. X-ray diffraction was investigated on COL-CDs using XRD-Rigaku unit using (λ = 1.5406) CuK, Ni-Filtered radiation. Horiba France micro-Raman spectrometer, LabRAM HR Evolution were used to study the graphite and disorder structures in the samples. Oppo F15 smartphone camera (48 + 8 + 2 + 2) were used to capture the digital images.

2.8 mL of phosphate buffer (pH 7.2) and 0.2 mL of COL-CDs were transferred into a quartz cuvette (3.5 mL) and performed spectrofluorimetry in the wavelength range of 350 – 600 nm. A known different concentration of L-Adr were added with the above solution. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity of the solution was recorded.

Commercially available intravenous sample (Adrenaline Injection I.P, 1 mg) were purchased from a nearby medical store. From the nearest clinical laboratory we have collected blood serum samples and used without any further purifications. Whatmann. No.1 filter paper was used to filter the collected human urine samples. Tap water is collected from our nearby places. Finally, a known concentration of L-Adr were spiked into the real sample containing COL- CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of reagent-less paper based sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To create a hydrophobic pattern on Avantech chromatography paper sheet (grade No.1, A4 size), Xerox colour Qube 8570 wax printer was used. The reagent-less wax-printed paper were placed in the oven for about 2 min at 100 °C. The printed wax on the paper was melted with by heating process and penetrates the substrate producing a hydrophobic wall (black) around the hydrophilic layer (white). After cooling the wax-printed paper at room temperature, the hydrophilic area were well defined. Further, 100 µL of COL-CDs into each well and allowed to dry at room temperature. Thus, reagent-less paper based sensor kit was used for on-site monitoring of L-Adr.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorbance and emission spectral studies of COL-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The absorbance peak of COL-CDs were obtained at 222 nm and 283 nm (Fig. 1 A). The observed COL-CDs peaks at 222 nm and 283 nm refer the transition of ᴨ → ᴨ* type of C=C (sp2) bond [24,25]. The Fig. 1B. refers the emission spectrum of COL-CDs at excitation wavelength of 340 nm and emission peak of COL-CDs was situated at 410 nm. The colour of COL-CDs is brown under daylight and blue colour under UV light (Fig. 1A. inset; (i) and (ii)).

The fluorescence properties of COL-CDs were also well examined at different excitation wavelength (Fig. 1B.). The excitation was varied from 300 to 380 nm, which has about 10 nm increment for each wavelength. The fluorescence intensity increases gradually with the increase in the excitation wavelength. The maximum intensity was observed at 410 nm for the excitation of 340 nm. Excitation wavelength from 350 to 420 nm the emission intensity was decreased slowly and shifting towards the higher wavelength (Fig. S1). This is one of the most common phenomenon exhibited by the carbon based fluorescent materials. Therefore, the synthesized COL-CDs clearly exhibits excitation-dependent fluorescence behaviour [26]. Further, the excitation wavelength dependent emission characteristic is attributed due to the diverse sizes of COL-CDs and dispersion of distinct surface defect states present in various functional groups on the surface of COL-CDs like hydroxyl, carboxyl and oxygen containing groups [27]. The COL-CDs obtained the maximum emission around 410 nm with an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. Thus, stokes shift was calculated to be 70 nm [28].

The nature of the chemical bonds of COL and COL-CDs were investigated by FT-IR spectroscopy. The FT-IR spectra of COL and COL-CDs are shown in Fig. 2 A; curve a and b. The chemical structure of COL shows a distinct peak at 3346 cm−1, which corresponds to the stretching vibrations of –OH and –NH. We observed a peak of –CH symmetrical stretching vibrations (νs) at 2917 cm−1. The peak position at 1535 cm−1 represents the bending vibration of –NH [29]. In addition the starching vibrations of C-O and C-O-C were observed at 1324 cm−1 and 1065 cm−1 respectively (Fig. 2A; curve a). The COL-CDs spectra gives the characteristic peaks of COL and can be noticed comparably. The (Fig. 2A; curve b) new broad peak appeared at 1633 cm−1 which corresponds to the characteristic peak of C=O, which is found to be observed only in COL-CDs but not observed in the FT-IR spectra of COL. Thus COL-CDs formed under hydrothermal condition possess abundant amount of oxygen, nitrogen containing groups. This indicates the successful formation of COL-CDs with various functional group.

The powder X-ray diffraction pattern were recorded for the COL-CDs (Fig. 2B). It was found to exhibit two prominent peaks (2θ) at 24.9 and 49.9, which is in agreement with the reported values [30]. Those diffraction peaks are corresponding to the planes of (002) and (100), respectively. From the obtained peaks it is seen that COL-CDs are amorphous in nature. The results ascribed the percentage of crystanallity in the synthesized COL-CDs were very low. With the help of Bragg’s Law lattice spacing (d) was calculated eqn. (1).
(1)
n λ
=
2 d
sin
θ
where ‘n’, ‘θ’ and ‘λ’ are corresponds to the order of diffraction, the interference of the Bragg’s angle and wavelength of X-ray radiation (1.5406 Å), respectively. The lattice spacing ‘d’ was calculated to be 0.35 nm for the lattice plane of (002) [31]. This calculated value is found to be larger than this interlayer spacing of bulk graphite carbon lattice 0.33 nm and this attributes to the poor crystanallity of the COL-CDs.

We have also investigated the morphology and the structure of one-pot synthesized COL-CDs with the help of HR-TEM imaging technique. The Fig. 3 A shows, the synthesized COL-CDs are found to be in spherical like structure and monodisperse in nature. It has particle size ranging from 1.9 to 5.42 nm. The Gaussian distribution curve fit shows the average particle size of COL-CDs. The calculated average particle size is 3.39 ± 0.08 nm (Fig. 3B) [32]. In addition, the lattice spacing of the COL-CDs was found to be 0.23 nm for the lattice plane of (100) [33]. These results obtained from HR-TEM and lattice spacing corresponds to the formation of carbon dots which has high dispersity between each particles.

Raman spectroscopy is a valuable technique to analyse the carbon dots. Raman spectra helps in providing the information about the vibrational modes and the surface defects of carbon dots. The Fig. 4 . exhibits Raman spectrum of COL-CDs peaks at 1328 cm−1 (D band) and 1599 cm−1 (G band).The observed peak at D band (sp3) relates to the (A1g) symmetry photon around K-zone boundary. The G band (sp2) relates to (E2g) vibrational mode of carbon dots [34,35]. IG and ID corresponds to the peak intensity of G and D bands, respectively. These are significantly related to the defect density and crystallite size. ID<IG, indicates the carbon dots are well graphitized and having many defects on the surface [36]. Thus the calculated ID/IG ratio of COL-CDs was found to be 1.08. This implies the disorder extent and sp3/sp2 carbon ratio. Therefore the fluorescent enhancement of COL-CDs have occurred due to surface defects [34].

Phosphate buffer with various pH condition (pH 1 to 12) was used to investigate pH effect on emission behaviour of COL-CDs (Fig. S2). The fluorescent intensity was found to increase gradually from pH (1 to 6). The fluorescent intensity was reached maximum at pH 7. Beyond pH 8, the emission behaviour started to drop down gradually [37]. The pH value varying from acidic and alkaline, is attributed due to the protonation and de-protonation of carboxyl group present on the surface of COL-CDs [38]. Therefore, we have fixed pH 7 as optimized pH for the other studies.

Moreover, we have also monitored the incubation time of COL-CDs fluorescence intensity in the presence of L-Adr. The Fig. S3 displays the fluorescence quenching of COL-CDs with the impact time in the presence of 0.59 µM of L-Adr. It can be noticed that the relative intensity of COL-CDs in the presence of 0.59 µM of L-Adr were enhanced from 1 to 3 min. After 3 min of time, relative fluorescent intensity in the sensor system was unaltered significantly. Therefore, the optimized response time were set to be 3 min for all further analysis.

William's comparative method was employed to determine the quantum yield value of COL-CDs. Quinine sulphate and COL-CDs solutions were prepared at six different concentrations, and their absorption and fluorescence spectra were measured. The obtained integrated fluorescence intensity and absorbance values were plotted as linear plot for both quinine sulphate and COL-CDs (Fig. S4 A and B) [39].
(2)
φ x
=
φ
s t
A
s t
A x
F x
F
s t
η
x
2
η
s t
2
where, φx and φst are the QY for COL-CDs and quinine sulphate, ηx and ηst are the refractive indices for COL-CDs and quinine sulphate solutions, respectively. Ast, Fst and Ax, Fx are absorbance and integrated fluorescence intensity of standard solution and COL-CDs, respectively. Based on the eqn (2), the quantum yield of COL-CDs found to be 27 %.

To investigate the sensitivity of COL-CDs, the various concentration of L-Adr were added to the known appropriate volume of COL-CDs. The COL-CDs exhibits emission peak at 410 nm for the excitation wavelength of 340 nm (Fig. 5 A; curve a). When the 0.24 µM L-Adr was added (Fig. 5A; curve b) the emission intensity was quenched noticeably at 410 nm. Moreover, when the concentration of L-Adr were increased from 0.59 to 33.6 µM, we have observed the gradual quenching of emission intensity at 410 nm (Fig. 5A curve b to t). Based on the quenching of the COL-CDs, we have calculated the concentration of L-Adr.

Additionally, COL-CDs shows blue emission under UV-light (365 nm). However, after the addition of 33.6 µM L-Adr the blue emission become non-emissive under UV-light (365 nm) (Fig. 5A; inset (i) and (ii)). The fluorescence emission of COL-CDs were changed from blue to colourless. These results, clearly indicates that the fluorescence intensity of COL-CDs is affected by the addition of different concentration of L-Adr. Based on the fluorescence quenching factor, we have calculated the concentration of L-Adr. The linear correlation curve were plotted against concentration of L-Adr Vs the emission intensity (Y=2.2027E10 X +8.07E5) of the COL-CDs in L-Adr concentration range of (a) 0, (b) 0.24, (c) 0.59, (d) 3.97, (e) 5.93, (f) 7.87, (g) 9.8, (h) 11.7, (i) 13.6, (j) 15.5, (k) 17.4, (l) 19.2, (m) 21.1, (n) 22.9, (o) 24.7 (p) 26.5 (q) 28.3 (r) 30.1 (s) 31.8 (t) 33.6 µM which has shown in Fig. 5B. Also the quantitative determination of L-Adr implies the limit of detection of COL-CDs sensor system was 1.08 nM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of COL-CDs in the presence of L-Adr
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–Vis absorption spectra of COL-CDs, COL-CDs spiked with L-Adr and L-Adr were recorded and shown in Fig. S5 and Fig. S6. There was no absorption band observed for L-Adr in the range of 300–600 nm. This clearly indicate, inner-filter effect is not responsible for the fluorescence quenching of COL-CDs with the L-Adr [40]. Further, we did not observed any blue or red-shift either in absorbance spectra before or after addition of L-Adr in COL-CDs. Therefore, the quenching effect of COL-CDs might be due to the interaction of the surface-bound organic molecules between the COL-CDs and L-Adr [41].

Further to elucidate the interaction between COL-CDs and L-Adr. In the Fig. S7; curve a and curve b represents the FT-IR spectra of COL-CDs and COL-CDs in the presence of L-Adr, respectively. From the obtained results, evidently clear from the COL-CDs reacted with L-Adr (Fig. S7; curve b). It can be noticed that the peaks of COL-CDs at 3346 cm− 1, 1628 cm−1, 1422 cm− 1 and 1324 cm− 1 intensity was weak significantly, which indicate corresponding groups (–COOH, –CONH2 and C-OH) were all participated in the reaction. Therefore photo-induced electron transfer (PET) is recognized as the primary mechanism responsible for the fluorescence quenching in COL-CDs by the quencher L-Adr [42]. PET is thermodynamically advantageous when the variation in Gibbs free energy (ΔG) is negative (ΔG<0) from based on Marcus theory. To found out the ΔG in our system, we have applied the Rehm-Weller equation.
(3)
E 00
= h ν
(4)
ν = c / λ

The excitation energy (E00) of the reactants, was calculated from the junction of the normalized emission and absorption spectra as shown in Fig. 6 A, and thus the E00 was determined to be 3.43 eV by using eqn. (3) and (4). The ΔG have calculated by the eqn. (4),
(5)
Δ G = e
E
OX
D
-
E
red
A
-
E 00
-
e 2
ε a
Here, “ν” represents the frequency, “c” is the light speed (approximately 3 × 108 m/s), “λ” is the wavelength at the intersection of the UV–Vis and fluorescence emission normalized spectra (λ = 360 nm), and “h” is the Planck constant (h ≈ 6.626 × 10-34 J⋅s). The Coulomb interaction energy associated with the solvent (e2/εa) is negligible in water. The initial single electron oxidation potential of the electron contributor (Eox(D)) and the corresponding reduction potential of single-electron of the electron acceptor (Ered(A)) can be evaluated through electrochemical approaches. CV curves indicates that the Ered(A) of COL-CDs is at −1.24 eV, and the Eox(D) of L-Adr is at + 1.30 eV (Fig. 6B and C). Ultimately, the calculated ΔG for the reaction between COL-CDs and L-Adr, using eqn. (5) is −0.89 eV, demonstrating that the transfer of electrons from L-Adr to the excited state COL-CDs is thermodynamically feasible. In this process, L-Adr acts as the electron donor, and COL-CDs function as the electron acceptor during PET process.

The selectivity of COL-CDs toward L-Adr versus other neurotransmitters and biomolecules were investigated and shown in Fig. 7 A. The addition of 2 mM of neurotransmitters like, L-dopa, dopamine, Nor-adrenaline, histamine, nicotinamide, creatinine and caffeine (330 folds) observed negligible changes in the emission intensity. Upon the addition of 4 mM potential interferences such as L-cysteine, ascorbic acid, L-glutamine, uric acid, glucose, lysine, citric acid (660-folds) to the COL-CDs system we have observed very negligible changes in the fluorescence emission intensity. Whereas, we observed quenching in the fluorescence emission intensity of COL-CDs by the addition of 5.93 µM concentration of L-Adr. Further, the fluorescence colour of the solution was also changed from blue to colourless when L-Adr was added to the COL-CDs system. There was no colour change while we added the above mentioned potential interferences into COL-CDs under UV-light (Fig. 7B). Thus, the presence of high concentration of potential interferences did not hinder the sensing capacity of COL-CDs towards L-Adr. These observation significantly confirms that the other mentioned interferences did not affect the fluorescent nature of COL-CDs. Therefore, the prepared sensor system has good selectivity towards L-Adr.

We utilized COL-CDs to determine L-Adr in the real sample such as intravenous liquid, clinical samples (blood serum and urine) and tap water. We did not find any variation in the emission intensity at 410 nm initially. Then the known volume of L-Adr were added in the samples in order to examine the found concentration and recoveries. From the calculation, good recovery percentage of the real sample were obtained in the range from 95.5 ± 0.4 to 101.1 ± 0.2. The obtained recovery shows that the COL-CDs system can sense L-Adr with good selectivity and reproducibility in the real samples (Table 2 ). Further, the results shows that COL-CDs can be used to detect L-Adr even in a complex environment with acceptable selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reagent-less paper based-kit for the sensing of L-Adr
ce:section.ce:para: The reagent-less paper-based kit were prepared for the on-site detection of L-Adr (Fig. 8 A). The fabrication details of the kit was given in experimental section 2.6. 25 µL of known concentration of L-Adr were dropped in COL-CDs modified reagent-less wax pencil designed paper based kit. For example (a) 0, (b) 0.24, (c) 3.97, (d) 7.87, (e) 11.7, (f) 15.5, (g) 19.2, (h) 21.1, (i) 22.9, (j) 26.5, (k) 30.1 µM of L-Adr were added in paper kit. After the addition of L-Adr the COL-CDs modified reagent-less paper based kit was well dried at room temperature for about 15 – 20 min. The COL-CDs coated paper kit shows blue colour under the UV-light, whereas the addition of L-Adr has quenched the fluorescence of COL-CDs under UV-light and act as “ON-OFF” sensor system.

Additionally with the help of colour picker app in the smartphone, we have captured the images of the above mentioned COL-CDs modified reagent-less paper with L-Adr spiked for further analysis. Thereby, we have uploaded the captured image and obtained the RGB intensity using colour picker app. The linear plot was drawn between obtained RGB intensity Vs concentration of L-Adr. The linear plot shows a good linearity with R2 = 0.9940 (Fig. 8B). This novel reagent-less paper based sensor kit has many advantages such as off-line data collection, no technical issues, flexible, affordability, reliability and has low environmental impacts for sensing of L-Adr in point-of-care analysis.

A novel one-pot synthesis of nitrogen-doped COL-CDs was derived from chitosan oligosaccharide lactate. The COL-CDs shows a very good excitation behaviour and it was well characterized by several techniques. Based on the fluorescence quenching factor the limit of detection was found to be 1.08 nM. Therefore, it act as a probe for a simple, rapid, sensitive method for the detection of L-Adr. The photo-induced electron transfer (PET) mechanism is the responsible for the fluorescence quenching of COL-CDs with L-Adr. Also, reagent-less paper based sensor kit was developed for the point-of-care detection of L-Adr using synthesized COL-CDs. The other neurotransmitters does not interrupt the detection of L-Adr. Furthermore, the COL-CDs was applied in real samples like intravenous liquid and clinical samples, which gives the good recovery percentage for L-Adr sensing. This is clearaly indicates the application of COL-CDs in complex environment.

Joseph Sartho Immaculate Mercy: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Pandiaraja Varatharajan: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Mohamad Hafiz Mamat: Supervision, Project administration, Investigation. S. Kutti Rani: Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Nagamalai Vasimalai: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: P. Varatharajan thanks to B. S. Abdur Rahman Crescent Institute of Science and Technology, Chennai-600 048, India, for the award of Junior Research Fellow under the University Fellowship (File No: Lr. No. 1443/Dean (R) 2022 Date: 22.11.2022).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2024.126011.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
